evidence_quotes.quote: When publishers shut down these servers, consumers are left with non-functional products they paid for.
Ban on post-purchase downgrades or functionality losses without user consent.

paragraphs.text: As a concerned EU citizen and long-time supporter of digital consumer rights, I urge the European Commission to enshrine stronger protections for digital video game ownership and preservation within the Digital Fairness Act and broader EU digital legislation. 1. Digital Ownership Must Be Meaningful When consumers purchase a digital video game, they reasonably expect permanent access to that productjust as they would with a physical copy. However, current licensing practices allow companies to revoke access arbitrarily, often without refund or recourse. Proposal: The EU should establish that digital game purchases constitute a form of ownership. This ownership must guarantee access beyond a publishers support timeline. 2. Video Games Are Art and Must Be Preserved Video games are a form of modern art. They represent the collective work of developers, writers, musicians, and visual artists. As with books, films, and music, they deserve cultural preservation. Proposal: The EU should recognize video games as cultural artifacts and ensure legal mechanisms are in place to prevent their erasure from history. 3. Server Shutdowns Must Not Invalidate Purchases Many modern games rely on online infrastructure for core functionality. When publishers shut down these servers, consumers are left with non-functional products they paid for. Proposal: Companies should be legally obligated to provide: An offline mode, or Official end-of-life server packages, or Release of the necessary server code/tools, OR Lose their exclusive right to block community-hosted private servers after service termination. This ensures that purchased content remains accessible and playable. 4. Protect Community Preservation Efforts In the absence of official support, consumers and developers often maintain abandoned games through community-run servers. Unfortunately, these efforts are frequently targeted with takedown notices. Proposal: If a game is no longer sold, supported, or playable via official means, companies must not be allowed to use copyright enforcement (e.g., DMCA equivalents) to shut down private preservation initiatives. 5. Legalize Access to Abandonware If a game is: No longer sold anywhere legally, No longer available from the publisher, And lacks official support or functionality, Then downloading or emulating it for personal use should be considered lawful under EU law. Proposal: The EU should introduce a legal framework for abandonware protection, including the lawful personal use of game ROMs and ISOs, when no legal path to access exists. 6. Ensure Consumer Protection from Arbitrary Revocation The ability of corporations to revoke digital products, alter license agreements post-purchase, or unilaterally remove access to purchased games is a serious breach of digital consumer trust. Proposal: Enact EU-wide digital product ownership protections, including: Clear guarantees of long-term access, Refund or alternative access in cases of product removal, Ban on post-purchase downgrades or functionality losses without user consent. ⸻ Conclusion Digital games are more than commercial productsthey are experiences, works of art, and cultural milestones. The EU must lead in protecting digital rights by: Ensuring that ownership is respected, Preserving games beyond their commercial life, And safeguarding the rights of individuals and communities who work to keep them alive. By integrating these principles into the Digital Fairness Act, the European Union can set a global standard for the ethical treatment of digital media and consumers.
